daily_brief: The day's global agenda is dominated by US President Donald Trump's arrival in Beijing for a high-stakes summit with Chinese President Xi Jinping, their first meeting since 2017, with discussions expected on trade, Taiwan, and the ongoing Iran conflict [BBC World, France 24, Nikkei Asia, Al Jazeera, Globe and Mail]. Trump downplayed differences with Xi over Iran before departing, though pressure mounts as the war enters its 75th day and Gulf tensions escalate over the Strait of Hormuz [Al Jazeera, Globe and Mail]. In Europe, UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer faces a political crisis as over 80 Labour MPs have called for his resignation following heavy local election losses, with Wednesday's King's Speech seen as a pivotal moment for his premiership [France 24, Al Jazeera, Financial Times]. The European Commission signaled new legislation to restrict children's access to social media could come within months, with President von der Leyen stating the debate over minimum age limits 'can no longer be ignored' [BBC Europe, RFI]. Meanwhile, Ukraine's anti-corruption agencies named former presidential chief of staff Andriy Yermak as a suspect in a money-laundering probe, marking a significant escalation in domestic accountability efforts [BBC Europe].

top_stories.headline: Trump Arrives in Beijing for High-Stakes Summit with Xi Amid Iran War and Trade Tensions
bullets: US President Donald Trump departed for Beijing on Tuesday for a three-day visit—the first by a US president to China since 2017—with formal talks with President Xi Jinping scheduled for Thursday and Friday
Agenda items include trade relations, US arms sales to Taiwan, artificial intelligence cooperation, and efforts to end the Iran conflict now in its 75th day

Trump downplayed Iran as a point of contention before departure, saying Xi has been 'relatively good,' while stating preventing Iran from acquiring nuclear weapons is his sole priority regardless of US economic impact
Trump is accompanied by corporate executives including Elon Musk, Tim Cook, and Jensen Huang, with expectations of commercial deals for aircraft and agricultural goods

sources.name: BBC World
sources.framing_note: Emphasizes China's increased strength and assertiveness since Trump's last visit, highlighting Xi's investments in renewable energy and AI

sources.name: France 24
sources.framing_note: Frames summit around multiple competing priorities—trade, Taiwan, Iran—noting few expect major breakthroughs

sources.name: Nikkei Asia
sources.framing_note: Focus on last-minute trade negotiations in Seoul and commercial deal expectations

sources.name: Al Jazeera
sources.framing_note: Highlights Taiwan as Beijing's top priority and frames Xi as predictable versus Trump's mercurial approach

sources.name: Globe and Mail
sources.framing_note: Emphasizes Trump's statement that Americans' financial struggles are 'not even a little bit' a factor in Iran negotiations

sources.name: Le Monde
sources.framing_note: Headline emphasizes Xi welcoming Trump 'from a position of strength'

top_stories.headline: UK Prime Minister Starmer Fights for Survival as Labour Revolt Deepens Ahead of King's Speech
bullets: Over 80 Labour MPs have called for Prime Minister Keir Starmer to resign following heavy losses in local and regional elections, though more than 100 others signed a statement backing him
Four junior ministers resigned on Tuesday as the party descended into open warfare over Starmer's leadership
Starmer held a brief meeting Wednesday morning with Health Secretary Wes Streeting, widely seen as a potential leadership rival
King Charles III will deliver the government's legislative agenda Wednesday, with Downing Street promising an 'ambitious programme' to make Britain 'stronger and fairer'

sources.name: France 24
sources.framing_note: Describes Starmer's job as 'hanging in the balance' and the speech as having 'huge repercussions'

sources.name: Al Jazeera
sources.framing_note: Frames Starmer as 'fighting for his political survival' and notes he has 'failed to deliver reforms'

sources.name: Financial Times
sources.framing_note: Headline describes Starmer as 'clings to power'

top_stories.headline: EU Moves Toward Restricting Children's Social Media Access, New Legislation Possible by Summer
bullets: European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen proposed a 'social media delay' for children and said new legislation could be put forward within months
An expert panel is due to deliver recommendations by July on strengthening protections for minors online, including examining potential bans for younger teenagers
Denmark and nine other EU member states including France are already pushing for various minimum age limits for platform access
Australia became the first country to ban social media for under-16s in December 2025; France aims to implement a ban for under-15s by September 2026

sources.name: BBC Europe
sources.framing_note: Straightforward policy reporting, notes von der Leyen 'did not rule out' a ban

sources.name: RFI English
sources.framing_note: Frames as 'fresh push to rein in' social media giants, emphasizes mental health concerns

top_stories.headline: Ukraine's Former Presidential Chief of Staff Yermak Named Suspect in Corruption Probe
bullets: Andriy Yermak, President Zelensky's former right-hand man, appeared in Kyiv court Tuesday after being named a suspect in a money-laundering scheme by Ukraine's two anti-corruption agencies
The allegations center on a $10.5 million luxury construction project outside Kyiv; prosecutors requested either preventive detention or bail of approximately $4 million
Yermak resigned as head of the presidential office last November following an anti-corruption raid on his flat; he denies wrongdoing and says he owns only 'one flat and one car'
The head of Ukraine's National Anti-corruption Bureau stressed that President Zelensky himself is not part of the investigation

sources.name: BBC Europe
sources.framing_note: Provides detailed factual account, notes Yermak led Ukraine's talks with the US until his resignation

top_stories.headline: WHO Says No Sign of Larger Hantavirus Outbreak as Cruise Ship Passengers Evacuated
bullets: The World Health Organization said there is 'no sign' of a larger hantavirus outbreak following evacuation of passengers from the cruise ship MV Hondius, though warned 'the situation could change'
Three people have died after traveling on the ship; nine cases are confirmed with two suspected, including a French woman in intensive care with 'the most severe form' of the disease
The final 28 passengers arrived in the Netherlands on Tuesday; 12 employees at a Dutch hospital are in quarantine after possible exposure while handling patient samples
Experts emphasized the virus has 'pretty low' transmission efficiency compared to COVID-19, though it has a fatality rate of approximately one in three

sources.name: BBC Europe
sources.framing_note: Balanced factual reporting on outbreak status and evacuation details

sources.name: France 24
sources.framing_note: Featured expert interviews emphasizing scientific differences from COVID-19 and pushing back against pandemic comparisons
